title: SEGMENT 10 — BLA versus NDA — gene therapy is regulated as a biologic

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — BLA versus NDA — gene therapy is regulated as a biologic
Welcome back.
I hope you got some coffee.

We are now into the second half of the lecture, and in this segment I want to address what might sound like a small distinction but is actually structurally important: the difference between a BLA and an NDA.
The NDA is the New Drug Application — the regulatory submission that traditional small-molecule drugs file at the end of development to seek approval from FDA.
NDAs are reviewed by CDER, the Center for Drug Evaluation and Research.
The regulatory framework for NDAs is built on the foundations of the Federal Food, Drug, and Cosmetic Act, originally passed in 1938.
The BLA is the Biologics License Application — the equivalent submission for biologic products.
BLAs are reviewed by CBER, the center we discussed in Segment 2, which is the home of gene therapy regulation.
The regulatory framework for BLAs is built on a different statutory foundation — the Public Health Service Act of 1944 — which gives FDA the authority to license biologic products.

The terminology even reflects this lineage: a biologic is licensed, not just approved, because the original framework was about licensing manufacturers of biological products like vaccines and antitoxins.
Gene therapy is regulated as a biologic.
You file a BLA, not an NDA.
You deal with CBER, not CDER.
That is the bottom line, and it has practical consequences.
Why does the distinction matter?
Three reasons.
First, the manufacturing oversight is more intensive for biologics.

Because biologic products are made in living systems, and because small changes in the manufacturing process can change the properties of the final product, CBER pays close attention to every step of how a gene therapy is made.
The agency may inspect manufacturing facilities before approval, and the BLA itself contains an enormous amount of detail about the manufacturing process that is not typical for small-molecule NDAs.
We will see in Segment 16 that CMC — chemistry, manufacturing, and controls — is often the limiting step for gene therapy.
The intensity of biologic-product manufacturing oversight is one reason why.
Second, the post-market commitments tend to be longer and more demanding for gene therapies.
For a small-molecule drug, post-market surveillance is mostly about catching rare adverse events that did not show up in clinical trials.

For a gene therapy, post-market surveillance is also about characterizing the long-term durability and safety of a one-time treatment — questions that, by definition, you cannot fully answer in a clinical trial of finite duration.
We will see in Segment 15 that the FDA expects up to fifteen years of follow-up for some AAV gene therapies, which is the longest post-market commitment in modern medicine.
Third, biologics have different exclusivity protections than small molecules.
For a small-molecule NDA, the standard data exclusivity period is five years.
For a biologic licensed via the BLA pathway, the standard data exclusivity period is twelve years.
That difference matters enormously to the commercial calculus of developing a gene therapy.

The longer exclusivity period reflects, in part, the more intensive development process — and it provides the financial runway that makes investing in a small-population gene therapy commercially viable.

So when you read in the news that a gene therapy has been "approved" — for instance, "Casgevy was approved by FDA in December 2023" — what has actually happened, in technical regulatory terms, is that CBER has issued a biologics license to the manufacturer, authorizing them to produce and market the product.
The license, like all FDA licenses, can be amended over time as new information emerges.
The manufacturer can be required to update labeling, conduct post-market studies, or in extreme cases withdraw the product.
The license is not a one-time event; it is the start of a continuing regulatory relationship.
The EU equivalent is a marketing authorization, issued by the European Commission on the basis of a favorable CHMP opinion based on a CAT recommendation.
The conceptual structure is similar — it is an authorization to market, subject to ongoing obligations — but the legal mechanics differ.
For both jurisdictions, "approval" is the start of the post-market phase, not the end of regulatory engagement.
